<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Clan Munakata]][[ja:宗像氏]][[simple:Munakata clan]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Clan Munakata]][[ja:宗像氏]][[simple:Munakata clan]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Clan Munakata]][[ja:宗像氏]][[simple:Munakata clan]]




{{Expand Japanese|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata}}
{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin={{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}}|home province={{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}}|founder=disputed original ancestor
possibly {{ill|Ame no Hohi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Hohi|qq=}}
historically first known {{ill|Kiyoshi Munakata|ja|宗形清氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyoshi Munakata|1=Munakata no Kiyouji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10205601}}|current head=|image=File:Honden of Munakata Grand Shrine (Hetsu Shrine).JPG|caption=Munakata Taisha Honden|parent house=[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]|image caption=Munakata Taisha Honden|alt=Munakata Taisha Honden}}

The {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|simple|User:Immanuelle/Munakata_clan|ja|宗像氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} are a Japanese clan that runs [[Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]]<ref name="Matsunaga">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.ph/08goA |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>. They had advanced {{ill|Sailing|en|Sailing|qid=Q192520|lt=Sailing|1=Sailing}} skills.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region |url=https://www.okinoshima-heritage.jp/en/know/people.html?path=%2Fen%2Fknow%2Fpeople.ctp |access-date=2023-12-01 |website=The Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region |language=en}}</ref> They are said to have the same origins as the {{ill|Izumo clan|en|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}} but care for the daughters of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] instead of him.<ref name="國學院大學デジタルミュージアム">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9149|access-date=2024-03-24|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref> 

They are buried in {{ill|Tsuyazaki Kofun Cluster#Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun Cluster|lt=Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun Cluster|ja|新原・奴山古墳群|en|Tsuyazaki Kofun Cluster#Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuyazaki Kofun Cluster#Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun Cluster|1=Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun Cluster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22119078}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region |url=https://www.okinoshima-heritage.jp/en/know/tradition.html?path=%2Fen%2Fknow%2Ftradition.ctp |access-date=2023-12-01 |website=The Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=The Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region|url=https://www.okinoshima-heritage.jp/en/know/shimbaru.html?path=%2Fen%2Fknow%2Fshimbaru.ctp|access-date=2024-03-20|website=The Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region|language=en}}</ref>

The clan had close ties with the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, engaging in intermarriages to establish alliances throughout the continent. Despite Japan ceasing its diplomatic missions to China, the Munakata Clan maintained trade with continental partners, asserting control over maritime {{ill|Trade|en|Trade|qid=Q601401|lt=Trade|1=Trade}} and rights.<ref name="Nogami">{{Cite web |last=Nogami  |first=Takahiro |title=Munakata Shinkō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8876 |url-status=live  |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>

Now, only shrine priests live on Okinoshima, an island with a large stone altar. From 1954 to 1972, research on the island found ancient ceremonies and objects in rocks, dating from the fourth to the ninth centuries. These discoveries helped understand early Shinto rituals and worship.<ref name="Nogami" />

Archaeologists on the island uncovered over 100,000 artifacts, from Japan and places like {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and {{ill|Iran|en|Iran|qid=Q794|lt=Iran|1=Iran}}. Many are National Treasures or {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. There are rules against talking about Okinoshima or taking anything from it.<ref name="Nogami" />

== Origins ==
The origins of the clan are unclear. Some sources say they descend from the traditional [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] of the region of a common origin with the {{ill|Izumo clan|en|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}},<ref name="國學院大學デジタルミュージアム" /><ref name=":0">https://www.munakata-archives.asia/Dat/bunken/0000000073_02.pdf</ref>{{rp|151-153}} and others say they are a cadet branch of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.

{{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|en:draft|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} is described as marrying [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] which links them in with the {{ill|Izumo clan|en|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}} in myth.<ref name="國學院大學デジタルミュージアム" /><ref name=":0" />{{rp|151-153}}

If they were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Kuni no miyatsuko]] and took on a priestly role at [[Munakata Taisha|Munakata Taisha]], they share this history with the {{ill|Aso clan|en|Aso clan|qid=Q11657653|lt=Aso clan|1=Aso clan}} of [[Aso Shrine|Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Owari clan|en|Owari clan|qid=Q11465311|lt=Owari clan|1=Owari clan}} of {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|qq=}}, the {{ill|Izumo clan|en|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}} of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo-taisha]],<ref name="Matsunaga" /> the {{ill|Amabe clan|en|Amabe clan|qid=Q65272445|lt=Amabe clan|1=Amabe clan}} of {{ill|Kono Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kono Shrine|qq=}}<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web|title=海部氏系図|trans-title=Amebe shikeizu|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777|publisher={{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agency for Cultural Affairs|qq=}}|language=Japanese|accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>, and the {{ill|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qid=Q11387078|lt=Yamato clan|1=Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko}} of [[Ōyamato Shrine|Ōyamato Shrine]].

The confusion may be due to an early interbreeding of them with the Imperial house.<ref name="國學院大學デジタルミュージアム" />
==Relationship with Izumo==
The origins of the Munakata clan, are not mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] or [[Nihon Shoki]]. The {{ill|Kujiki|en|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Kujiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujiki|qq=}} provides the origin, stating that the Munakata clan was descended from [[Ōkuninushi]] through Atada-Katasumi-no-Mikoto. This connection suggests a descent from [[Ōkuninushi]], despite the deity being a god rather than a goddess, indicating possible later interpretations.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|151-153}}

The Munakata clan claimed Izumo lineage, reflected in legends such as the birth of the Three Goddesses of Munakata from the ukei between [[Amaterasu]] and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], and the marriage of [[Ōkuninushi]] to {{ill|Tagorihime|ja|タキリビメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takiribime|en:draft|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, who resided in Munakata Okutsu-Miya.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|151-153}}

Similarities between Munakata and Izumo include administrative and religious roles, as seen in the analogy between the administrators of Munakata district and Ou district, and the Kokuzo of Munakata and Izumo. The Nihonshoki recounts an episode during {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}'s reign where Izumo-Furune, responsible for a sacred treasure, is involved in a fratricidal conflict, linking {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|qid=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}} (Munakata district in Chikuzen) with {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|151-153}}

[[Munakata Taisha]] is the only shrine in the [[Engishiki]] on the coast of the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|qid=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}. This area contains archaeological sites with Kofuns similar to those in northern Kyūshū, suggesting a connection or settlement by people from the Munakata region.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|151-153}}

There as a trade route from Munakata to {{ill|Tsuruga, Fukui|en|Tsuruga, Fukui|qid=Q847581|lt=Tsuruga|1=Tsuruga, Fukui}}, which stopped in {{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|qid=Q301580|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo}}, which was important for controlling Japan. The Okinaga clan from {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} may have sailed on this route, explaining the imperial family's focus on sea traffic and Kyūshū during the 7th century.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|151-153}}
==Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun Cluster==
The [[Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun Cluster|Tsuyazaki Kofun Cluster]] was constructed in the mid-late Kofun period, from the late 5th century to the late 6th century. In 2017, it was registered as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} as one of the constituent assets of the {{ill|Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region|qq=}}.<ref name="Bunka3">{{cite web|url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/decisions/6891|title=Decision 41 COM 8B.19 Sacred Island of Okinoshima and Associated Sites in the Munakata Region (Japan)|language=en |publisher=UNESCO |accessdate=December 20, 2023|qq=draft}}</ref>  It was the only portion of the Tsuyazaki Kofun Cluster to be included in the World Heritage Site designation. The Shinbaru-Nuyama cluster consists of five keyhole-shaped tumuli, one square tumulus, and 41 round tumuli, all of which are serially numbered. In addition, records indicate there were 18 other Kofuns that have lost due to the excavation of surrounding {{ill|Paddy field|en|Paddy field|qid=Q842623|lt=Paddy field|1=Paddy field}}s. At the time the tumuli was built, there was an inlet on the west side of the hill, but it gradually receded, and in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} it was reclaimed and turned into a salt field, and after the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, it became a paddy field. There are reservoirs scattered around the tumulus group, but these were built for irrigation in the early modern period and have nothing to do with the tumulus. The hilly area is divided by {{ill|Japan National Route 495|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan National Route 495|qq=}}. The {{ill|Basalt|en|Basalt|qid=Q43338|lt=Basalt|1=Basalt}} used in the stone burial chambers of the kofun was transported by ship from Ainoshima in the {{ill|Genkai Sea|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genkai Sea|qq=}}.

<gallery perrow="4" widths="175px" heights="175px">
File:新原・奴山古墳群20号墳.JPG|Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun No.20
File:新原・奴山古墳群21号墳.JPG|Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun No.21
File:新原・奴山古墳群22号墳.JPG|Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun No.22
File:新原・奴山古墳群34～36号墳.JPG|Shinbaru-Nuyama Kofun No.34～36
</gallery>

==List of Munakata high priests==

'''Munakata Daiguushi''' (宗像大宮司) refers to the chief priest of {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|Munakata Taisha|qid=Q704962|lt=Munakata Taisha|1=Munakata Taisha}} located in present‐day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} {{ill|Munakata, Fukuoka|en|Munakata, Fukuoka|qid=Q819603|lt=Munakata City|1=Munakata, Fukuoka}}. The office of Daiguushi was exclusively held by the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} until the main line of the Munakata Daiguushi family became extinct; nowadays the shrines have been divided into three.

== List of Munakata Daiguushi ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Order !! Name !! Tenure Period !! Notes
|-
| align="center" | 1 || {{ill|Sonomata Kiyoshi|ja|宗形清氏|lt=Kiyoshi|lt_ja=清氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonomata Kiyoshi|1=Munakata no Kiyouji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10205601}} || {{ill|Engi (era)|en|Engi (era)|qid=Q1374325|lt=Engi|1=Engi (era)}} 14 ({{ill|914|en|914|qid=Q24855|lt=914|1=914}}) – {{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|qid=Q1327340|lt=Tenkō|1=Tengyō}} 1 ({{ill|938|en|938|qid=Q24794|lt=938|1=938}}) || Tradition holds he was a son of {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|qid=Q349267|lt=Utatennō|1=Emperor Uda}}.

|-

|-
| align="center" | 17 || {{ill|Sonomata Ujihira|ja|宗形氏平|lt=Ujihira|lt_ja=氏平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonomata Ujihira|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Tenshō 1 (1131) || 
|-

| align="center" | 77(76) || {{ill|Munakata Ujitsugu|ja|宗像氏続|lt=Ujitsugu|lt_ja=氏続|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Ujitsugu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || {{ill|Eishō (Muromachi period)|en|Eishō (Muromachi period)|qid=Q1193577|lt=Eishō|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 2 ({{ill|1505|en|1505|qid=Q6153|lt=1505|1=1505}}) ||

| align="center" | 78(77) || {{ill|Munakata Tadashi? (Masashi)|ja|宗像正氏|lt=Tadashi? (Masashi)|lt_ja=正氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Tadashi? (Masashi)|1=Munakata Masauji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11451864}} || {{ill|Eishō (Muromachi period)|en|Eishō (Muromachi period)|qid=Q1193577|lt=Eishō|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 3 ({{ill|1506|en|1506|qid=Q6154|lt=1506|1=1506}}) – {{ill|Tenmon|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmon|qq=}} 15 ({{ill|1546|en|1546|qid=Q6533|lt=1546|1=1546}}) || [[Yamada Incident]]
|-
| align="center" | 79(78) || {{ill|Munakata Ujiotoko|ja|宗像氏男|lt=Ujiotoko|lt_ja=氏男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Ujiotoko|1=Munakata Ujio|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11451870}} || {{ill|Tenmon|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmon|qq=}} 16 ({{ill|1547|en|1547|qid=Q6547|lt=1547|1=1547}}) – Tenmon 20 ({{ill|1551|en|1551|qid=Q6560|lt=1551|1=1551}}) || 
|-
| align="center" | 80(79) || {{ill|Munakata Shige|ja|宗像氏貞|lt=Shige|lt_ja=氏貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shige|1=Munakata Ujisada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10204112}} || Tenmon 21 ({{ill|1552|en|1552|qid=Q6565|lt=1552|1=1552}}) – {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qid=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 14 ({{ill|1586|en|1586|qid=Q6739|lt=1586|1=1586}}) || Main Munakata Daiguushi line became extinct; Munakata clan status was altered
|-
| align="center" | || {{ill|Munakata Munafusa|ja|宗像宗繁|lt=Munafusa|lt_ja=宗繁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Munafusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || One of the chief priests after the extinction of the main line || 
|-
| align="center" | || {{ill|Munakata Tatsumi|ja|宗像辰美|lt=Tatsumi|lt_ja=辰美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Tatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Same as above || 
|}
* It is more commonly asserted that there were only 79 generations of Munakata Daiguushi.
